pages_preview.text_preview: Pre-Analysis Plan on “Inequality and social preferences - an experimental study with register data” Michael Kirchler, Kaisa Kotakorpi, Peter H. Matthews, Satu Metsälampi, Topi Miettinen, Xiaogeng Xu 1. Introduction In this study, we investigate the impact of inequality on cooperation, trust, and social preferences. We cooperate with Statistic Finland (SF) and run an artefactual field experiment with a sample from the Finnish population. In particular, we let subjects know about their real-world rank in the distribution of disposable income andinform them about the real-world income group in the distribution of the others they play with. We then let them interact in a series of games to examine the impact of inequality, measured by differences in real-world income rank, on social preferences in a between- and within-subjects design. In particular, we investigate the impact of real-world income inequality on trust, altruism and cooperation behavior, and beliefs about these. Thus, with our project protocol, we analyze the impact of real-world inequality on societally relevant preferences, behavior, and beliefs. With respect to existing literature (e.g., Suchon and Teroude, 2022; Balietti et al., 2023), our innovation is (i) to use controlled real-world inequality by relying on differences in the respondents’ rank in the income distribution, (ii) to run the experiment with people from the general population instead of student subjects and (iii) to let subjects interact with others from different income groups. The design of this study answers the following primary research questions: - How do Public Good contributions (cooperation) depend on income rank(s) / inequality? - How does trust (combined effect of trust + altruism / social preference) depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How does trustworthiness depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How do beliefs about trustworthiness depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How does altruism (social preference) depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How do beliefs about altruism depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How does positive reciprocity (difference between trustworthiness and altruism) depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? - How do beliefs about positive reciprocity depend on the income rank(s) / inequality? 2. Tasks We focus on the following tasks: (ia) binary allocation tasks, (ib) belief elicitations regarding these, (iia) Player B choice in two-player (sequential) trust game, (iib) belief elicitations regarding these, (iic) Player A choice in two-player (sequential) trust game and (iii) three-player public good game (3PGG). All participants take all tasks. This means that 1

subjects go through all respective tasks (i.e., (ia) until (iii)) in a within-subjects design. Also, subjects take decisions in different income rank (bin) compositions in a within-subjects design. Due to the impossibility of simultaneous play in general population subject pools handled with national statistical offices, we run all games in the strategy method. The order of tasks to be played is randomized for each individual at the start of the experiment. The tasks are grouped in three blocks: Block 1 (tasks ia, ib), Block 2 (iia, iib, iic) and Block 3 (task iii). The blocks as well as the tasks within a block are in random order. Each participant completes these 6 tasks where earnings depend not only on their own choices but also the choices of others: trust game tasks (iia)-(iic), corresponding allocation tasks (ia)-(ib), and a three-player public good game 3PGG (iii). In the sequential trust game, Player A moves first (iic), and Player B moves thereafter (iia), conditioning her/his choice on the outcome of the choice of Player A. If the outcome of A’s choice is “aside”, then each player receives 300 irrespective of what B does, i.e. (300,300). If the outcome of A’s choice is “forward”, then Player B gets to decide between “aside” or “forward”, and (“forward”, “forward”) leads to (600,600) and (“forward”, “aside”) leads to (0, 900) where Player A payoff is listed first. This is a binary trust game version of the baseline game. Task (iib) elicits beliefs about task (iia). In allocation tasks (i), there is an active decision maker and a passive recipient. There are two variants of the allocation tasks: in (ia), Player B decides between allocation (600,600) and (0,900). In task (ib), the participant is asked to predict the percentage of outcomes (600,600) and (0,900) in task (ia). Likewise, the trust game Player B decision (iia), conditioning on Player A “forward”, can be compared to the allocation task (ia), the difference measuring positive reciprocity and the belief difference measuring the beliefs about positive reciprocity (Bohnet et al. 2008; Cox et al. 2008; Engelmann et al. 2023). The 3-player 3PGG is set up as follows: each player decides whether to move the sum “aside” or to “forward”. If Player A moves “aside”, she/he receives 300. If Player A moves forward, the amount is doubled and divided between Player B and Player C who thus receive 300 each. Player B and C make identical decisions simultaneously. The possible outcomes are thus (600,600,600), (300,300,300), (300,300,900) (and all its permutations), (600,600,0) (and all its permutations). Below, we indicate the dependent variables in our analysis from broader to more narrow. In particular, for each bin (or for each percentile), we study how each measure depends on the rank-bin of other players. Gross measures - Public good provision: forward-choice in 3-player PGG (iii). - (Gross) trust: Player A forward-choice in the trust game (iic). - Altruism/efficiency: Player A forward-choice in (ia). - Trustworthiness: forward-choice in (iia). Beliefs 2

- Belief about altruism: beliefs about Player B choice in allocation task (ib). - Belief about trustworthiness: the belief about Player B-reaction to forward-choice by A in the trust game (iib). Net measures - Positive reciprocity: difference in forward choice between (iia) and (ia). - Beliefs about positive reciprocity: difference between (iib) and (ib). 3. Treatments and Experimental Procedure The key exogenous variation we are interested in concerns the income ranks of the matched players/participants. We partition participants into three real-world income rank bins (derived from subjects’ income data from Statistic Finland), i.e. income groups: the lowest income 50% of the participants (bottom 50%), the highest income 10% of the participants (top 10%), and those with income higher than the bottom 50% and lower income than the top 10% (50%-90%). In the treatment condition, the participant learns her own income percentile in the age reference group (among Finnish individuals born in the same year). We will exogenously regulate information about the income group of the other matched participant(s). Rank information treatment: The participant learns the income group of the matched participant(s). The income group is based on the matched participant’s income rank percentile among individuals born in the same year as the responding participant. Thus, within each game, each decision maker has to make three decisions, i.e. with people from each income group as the respective other player. No information treatment (Control): Subjects play the games without knowing the income group of the others, and without information on their own rank in the age distribution. The control group participants skip entirely stage 2 where beliefs about their own rank are elicited and the information is revealed. As a consequence, the following comparisons are obtained: Symmetric rank information: individuals are connected with others of the same bin. Upward asymmetry: someone is matched with a person of higher rank. Here, someone can be matched with others that are up to two bins further up. Of course, upward asymmetry does not apply to the Top 10%. Downward asymmetry: This is the mirror image of upward asymmetry and, consequently, someone can be matched with others down from one bin to two bins. Of course, downward asymmetry does not apply to the bottom 50%. In the Rank information treatment, ● choices in the sequence i and ii are elicited with a choice list method ○ ASIDE or FORWARD when the other player come from each bin Bottom: Middle Top: 3

● beliefs in the sequence i and ii are elicited with a choice list method. Player A of the situation belongs to the same income group as the participant from which belief is elicited; and Player B belongs to bottom-50%, upper-middle 50-90%, and top-10%, respectively. Out of 100 randomly drawn participants in each bin, how many choose FORWARD Bottom: __ choose Middle: __ choose Top: __ choose forward and __ choose forward and __ choose forward and __ choose aside aside aside ● the 3PGG choices are elicited with a choice list / matrix method (specific frame of the strategy method): ASIDE or FORWARD when the other two players come from bins Bottom-Bottom XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX Bottom-Middle Middle-Middle XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX Bottom-Top Middle-Top Top-Top In any belief elicitation outlined above, we ask the participants to evaluate the percentage of outcomes of the (independent) decisions of others in a particular context / decision node: how likely are the two outcomes (“forward”/”aside”) in a sample of 100 randomly drawn participants (in the relevant income rank bin). This belief elicitation is implemented in a simple and transparent frequentist manner using the method proposed by Schlag and Tremewan (2021), which circumvents the complexity issues identified by Danz et al. (2022) related to binary scoring rule and quadratic scoring rule: “We randomly draw 100 people from among the participants in this study. Your task is to make a guess regarding the choices of these participants. The guess is correct if it falls into the same ten [Finnish “kymmenluku” understood as 0-9, 10-19, 20-29, etc.] as the actual, observed proportion of the decisions of the participants in this study. In your estimation, when making a decision in the role of the purple participant, how many of the hundred participants will move ASIDE and how many FORWARD?” Figure 1 outlines the flow of action for subjects entering the experiment, showing the between- and within-subjects characteristics. Links to the otree codes of the entire experiment and the screenshots of the choice screens can be found on OSF: https://osf.io/psbd4/. The entire experiment is programmed in otree and all steps in the instructions will be on-screen. 4

Figure 1: Flow of action for participant i entering the experiment. 4. Sampling Frame / Target Population SF will invite subjects based on the following pre-screening characteristics: ● Birth year: 1975-1985 ● Language: Finnish or Swedish ● Municipality of residence in Finland is not in Åland Islands ● The individual has resided in Finland for at least 10 years before the survey experiment in 2022 (described in Xu et al., 2023) and has a Finnish social security number. ● They are not permanently out of the labor force. ● The highest earned educational degree (highest degree) is not "unknown." ● The family status of the individual is not “child.” SF will invite two samples: Sample 1: Includes all individuals who completed the survey experiment conducted in 2021 (as described in Xu et al., 2023) and did not request removal from the participant pool. Additionally, individuals no longer meeting the criteria of the sampling frame (e.g., those who 5

moved abroad since 2022) are excluded. Using this sample allows us to study the correlations between interactive behavior and the attitude, preference, and belief variables elicited in the 2021 collection. Sample 2: Corresponds to Sample 1 with respect to sampling criteria but consists of individuals who did not take part in the survey experiment in 2021. SF draws the samples using weighted sampling to account for observed unit non-response among lower-educated individuals in the pilot study for the 2021 survey experiment (Xu et al., 2023). The target number of respondents is 6,800 individuals in total (approximately 3,110 individuals in Sample 1 and 3,690 in Sample 2). The total number of invitees is 19041, and they are randomized into treatments so that 9523 (50%) in the rank information treatment and 9518 (50%) in the control treatment. 5. Payment-matching Upon invitation, subjects will receive a personal access link to the experimental software webpage, where they can run the experiment anytime they want. Payment-matching in all tasks and calculations of payments are done ex-post. Compensation will be paid to the subjects within four months after the start of the data collection. The payments are handled by the staff at Hanken (who are not part of the research team). We will draw 5 participants at random. For each of the participants, we will draw a relevant decision (not including the belief elicitation questions). We will draw matched participants for this decision maker in the situation from the same treatment group (control or relative income); each participant in the treatment is equally likely to be drawn. In the rank information treatment, we will check the income groups of the two (or three in 3PGG) thereby matched participants and determine the payoff-relevant choices of the drawn participants. We will then implement the choices and the implied payoffs accordingly. Those who are not drawn (to be paid based on their choices) are eligible to be paid if their guesses about others’ behavior or their income rank are correct. We will randomly draw 50 beliefs out of all correct beliefs. Each participant whose correct belief was drawn will receive 100 eur. A participant can receive the 100 eur payment for only one correct belief. 6. Sociodemographic Variables and Survey Questions: After subjects made all decisions in the experiment, we ask survey questions, serving as explanatory variables (associations) of subjects’ decisions in the experiment. We will retest some measures we already measured in data collection in 2021 (see Xu et al., 2023) and pose some new questions. In addition, we will test the persistence of the income rank information treatments in round 1 (Xu et al., 2023) by eliciting beliefs about their income rank in 2021 among people born in the same year as them from respondents in the rank information treatment (unlike other questions, this question is asked before the choice tasks, see Figure 1). 6
